---
title: "Ch 9 — Circles"
subtitle: "Chords, arcs, angles, and the cyclic quadrilateral"
lesson-id: "cbse9.circles"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 9
ncert-chapter-title: "Circles"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse9.lines-and-angles", "t1.shapes-angles"]
unlocks: ["cbse10.circles-tangents", "cbse10.areas-related-to-circles"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you state the angle-sum theorem of a triangle? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse9.lines-and-angles](../06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you prove triangles congruent by SSS / SAS / ASA / RHS?
   *(If no, revisit
   [cbse9.triangles](../07-triangles/index.qmd).)*
3. Do you know "radius", "diameter", "circumference"? *(If no, revisit
   [t1.shapes-angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd).)*

Three yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a circular plate. Place a long ruler across the plate so it crosses
two points on the rim. Mark those points $A$ and $B$ — that's a
**chord**. Find the **midpoint** of $AB$, then drop a perpendicular from
the centre $O$ of the plate down to the chord. Where does it land?

```{ojs}
viewof chordPos = Inputs.range([-80, 80], {value: 30, step: 5, label: "chord offset"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const cx = 150, cy = 130, R = 100;
  const yOff = chordPos;
  if (Math.abs(yOff) > R - 1) return html`<div>chord misses circle</div>`;
  const halfLen = Math.sqrt(R*R - yOff*yOff);
  const ax = cx - halfLen, ay = cy + yOff;
  const bx = cx + halfLen, by = cy + yOff;
  const mx = (ax+bx)/2, my = (ay+by)/2;
  return html`<svg width="300" height="280" style="border:1px solid #ccc">
    <circle cx="${cx}" cy="${cy}" r="${R}" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <line x1="${ax}" y1="${ay}" x2="${bx}" y2="${by}" stroke="#36a" stroke-width="2"/>
    <line x1="${cx}" y1="${cy}" x2="${mx}" y2="${my}" stroke="#a30" stroke-dasharray="3,3"/>
    <circle cx="${cx}" cy="${cy}" r="3" fill="#000"/>
    <circle cx="${mx}" cy="${my}" r="3" fill="#a30"/>
    <text x="${cx + 6}" y="${cy - 6}" font-size="13">O</text>
    <text x="${mx + 6}" y="${my - 6}" font-size="13">M</text>
    <text x="${ax - 14}" y="${ay + 4}" font-size="13">A</text>
    <text x="${bx + 4}" y="${by + 4}" font-size="13">B</text>
  </svg>`;
}
```

Drag the slider. The dashed line from $O$ always hits the chord at its
midpoint $M$ at $90^\circ$. Move the chord — the perpendicular from
centre never misses the midpoint.

## Wonder

1. The radius from $O$ to a chord's midpoint is always perpendicular to
   the chord. Why must that be? Could a circle ever cheat this?
2. Cut two chords of equal length on the same circle. Are they equally
   far from the centre? What if you cut one short and one long?
3. Pick two points $A, B$ on a circle. Pick a third point $P$ anywhere
   on the *major* arc between them. The angle $\angle APB$ has a value.
   Slide $P$ along the major arc — does the value change?
4. A four-cornered figure with all four corners on the circle — what
   special relationship do its opposite angles share?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q3 is the **angle in the same segment** theorem (also called the
inscribed-angle theorem in this special form). Q4 is **cyclic
quadrilateral** — opposite angles sum to $180^\circ$.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 (Equal chords ⟺ equal distances)

In a circle with centre $O$, two chords $AB$ and $CD$ are drawn. $M$ is
the midpoint of $AB$ and $N$ is the midpoint of $CD$. Suppose $AB = CD$.
Prove $OM = ON$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use right-angled triangles $\triangle OMB$ and $\triangle OND$, with
the radius as hypotenuse.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $OM \perp AB$ and $ON \perp CD$. | Radius to midpoint of chord is perpendicular. |
| 2. $OB = OD$. | Both are radii. |
| 3. $MB = \tfrac{1}{2} AB$, $ND = \tfrac{1}{2} CD$, and $AB = CD$. | Given + midpoint. |
| 4. So $MB = ND$. | From 3. |
| 5. $\triangle OMB \cong \triangle OND$. | RHS (right angle, hypotenuse, side). |
| 6. $OM = ON$. | CPCT. $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

### Problem 2 (Angle subtended at centre vs at arc)

In a circle with centre $O$, an arc $AB$ subtends $\angle AOB$ at the
centre. Pick any point $P$ on the major arc. Use the radii $OA, OB, OP$
and the isosceles triangles $\triangle OPA$, $\triangle OPB$ to show
$\angle APB = \tfrac{1}{2} \angle AOB$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each radius makes an isosceles triangle with the chord through $P$.
Exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two opposite interior
angles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Join $PO$ and extend it to meet the circle at $Q$ (the diameter
through $P$). Look at $\triangle OPA$: it is isosceles ($OP = OA$), so
$\angle OAP = \angle OPA$. The exterior angle $\angle AOQ$ at $O$
equals $\angle OAP + \angle OPA = 2 \angle OPA$. Similarly
$\angle BOQ = 2 \angle OPB$. Adding (or subtracting, depending on which
side of $PO$ the points lie),
$\angle AOB = 2(\angle OPA + \angle OPB) = 2 \angle APB$.
$\blacksquare$

**The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is twice the angle it
subtends at any point on the major arc.**
</details>

### Problem 3 (Same-segment angles equal)

Two points $P_1$ and $P_2$ lie on the major arc of $AB$. Use the result
of Problem 2 to conclude $\angle AP_1 B = \angle AP_2 B$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both equal $\tfrac{1}{2} \angle AOB$ (the central angle of $AB$ is the
*same* angle for the same arc). So they equal each other.
**Angles in the same segment are equal.** $\blacksquare$
</details>

### Problem 4 (Angle in a semicircle)

If $AB$ is a **diameter** and $P$ is any point on the
[circle](/glossary.qmd#circle) (not $A$ or $B$), what is $\angle APB$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The arc $AB$ is now half the circle. The central angle is what?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle AOB = 180^\circ$ (since $AB$ is a diameter and $O$ is on it).
By the central-vs-arc theorem, $\angle APB = \tfrac{1}{2}(180^\circ) =
90^\circ$. **The angle in a semicircle is a [right
angle](/glossary.qmd#right-angle).** (This is **Thales' theorem**, the
oldest named theorem in geometry, ~600 BCE.)
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Thales of Miletus** (~624 – ~546 BCE) is the first person in
recorded history credited with *proving* a geometric statement rather
than just observing it. The legend has him deducing the height of the
Egyptian pyramids by comparing their shadow to a stick's shadow at the
same hour — a similar-triangles argument that exploits the very
inscribed-angle ideas you are deriving here. Two and a half millennia
later, his theorem still survives every geometry curriculum on Earth.
:::

### Problem 5 (Cyclic quadrilateral)

$ABCD$ is a quadrilateral with all four vertices on a circle (a *cyclic
quadrilateral*). Show $\angle A + \angle C = 180^\circ$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle A$ stands on the major arc of $BCD$; $\angle C$ stands on the
opposite arc.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle A$ is the inscribed angle on arc $BCD$, so it equals half the
central angle on arc $BCD$. Similarly $\angle C$ is half the central
angle on arc $BAD$. The two arcs together make the whole circle, so the
two central angles sum to $360^\circ$, hence the two inscribed angles
sum to $\tfrac{1}{2}(360^\circ) = 180^\circ$.
**Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary.**
$\blacksquare$
</details>

You discovered: chords pair up by length and distance from centre;
central angle is twice the inscribed angle for the same arc; angles in
the same segment are equal; the angle in a semicircle is right; and
opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to $180^\circ$.

::: {.key-insight}
The whole chapter is one theorem in disguise: **the central angle is
twice the inscribed angle on the same arc**. Equal chords ↔ equal
distances, same-segment angles, the semicircle right angle, the cyclic
quadrilateral — every result you proved is a special case or
consequence of that single fact.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

In a circle with centre $O$:

- **Chord, radius, diameter, arc, sector, segment** — standard
  vocabulary from the figure.
- **Theorem 1.** The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a
  chord bisects the chord. (Conversely, the line from the centre to
  the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to it.)
- **Theorem 2.** Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the
  centre, and conversely.
- **Theorem 3.** The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is twice
  the angle it subtends at any point on the major arc.
- **Theorem 4 (same segment).** Angles in the same segment are equal.
- **Theorem 5 (Thales).** The angle in a semicircle is a right angle.
- **Theorem 6 (cyclic quadrilateral).** The sum of either pair of
  opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is $180^\circ$.

:::

A short verse:

> *Drop from the centre — perpendicular and midpoint, both at once.*
> *Equal chords, equal distances; equal arcs, equal angles.*
> *Centre angle is double the rim;*
> *On a diameter, every angle is right.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> $AB$ is a chord of a circle of radius $5$ cm. The chord is at a
> perpendicular distance of $3$ cm from the centre. Find the length of
> $AB$.

A competent solver:

1. Drop the perpendicular from $O$ to $AB$, meeting at $M$. By Theorem
   1, $M$ is the midpoint of $AB$, and $OM = 3$, $OA = 5$.
2. Triangle $OMA$ is right-angled at $M$. By Pythagoras,
   $AM^2 + OM^2 = OA^2 \Rightarrow AM^2 = 25 - 9 = 16 \Rightarrow AM
   = 4$.
3. $AB = 2 \cdot AM = 8$ cm.

**Answer: $AB = 8$ cm.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A chord of length $24$ cm is at a perpendicular distance of $5$
cm from the centre. Find the radius.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same setup as the worked example, going the other way.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Half-chord = $12$. Radius $= \sqrt{12^2 + 5^2} = 13$ cm.
</details>

**W2.** $\angle AOB = 80^\circ$ at the centre of a circle. What is the
inscribed angle $\angle APB$ at any point $P$ on the major arc?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{1}{2}(80) = 40^\circ$.
</details>

**W3.** $ABCD$ is cyclic with $\angle A = 92^\circ$. Find $\angle C$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle C = 180 - 92 = 88^\circ$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Two chords $AB$ and $CD$ of a circle are equal in length. Show
that the arcs $AB$ and $CD$ are equal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre. Equal central angles
mean equal arcs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $OA = OC$ and $OB = OD$. | All radii. |
| 2. $AB = CD$. | Given. |
| 3. $\triangle OAB \cong \triangle OCD$. | SSS. |
| 4. $\angle AOB = \angle COD$. | CPCT. |
| 5. So arc $AB$ = arc $CD$. | Equal central angles cut equal arcs. $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

**P2.** $ABCD$ is a cyclic quadrilateral. The bisectors of $\angle A$
and $\angle C$ meet at point $X$. Show $X$ lies on the line $BD$ (or
state the relationship between the bisectors).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle A + \angle C = 180^\circ$, so half each sums to $90^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Half-angles sum to $90^\circ$, so the two bisectors are perpendicular at
their intersection. The full proof of "$X$ lies on $BD$" requires
additional configuration — the cleaner statement is that the bisectors
are perpendicular. Treat this as practice in extracting facts.
</details>

**P3.** A diameter of a circle is $26$ cm. A chord of the circle is $24$
cm long. Find its perpendicular distance from the centre.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Radius $= 13$. Half-chord $= 12$. Distance from centre $=
\sqrt{13^2 - 12^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5$ cm.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** $AB$ is a diameter and $CD$ is any other chord of a circle. The
perpendicular from $A$ to $CD$ has foot $P$; the perpendicular from $B$
to $CD$ has foot $Q$. Prove $CP = QD$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Drop a perpendicular from the centre $O$ to $CD$ — call the foot $M$.
$M$ is the midpoint of $CD$, and $OM$ is the average of $AP$ and $BQ$
(parallel lines, like rungs of a ladder).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AP, OM, BQ$ are all perpendicular to $CD$, so they are parallel. $O$
is the midpoint of $AB$ (centre on diameter), so $OM$ is the average of
$AP$ and $BQ$ — meaning $M$ is the midpoint of $PQ$. But $M$ is also
the midpoint of $CD$ (perpendicular from centre). So
$CP = CM - PM = MD - MQ = QD$. $\blacksquare$
</details>

**S2.** A circle has two parallel chords of lengths $6$ cm and $8$ cm.
The chords are on opposite sides of the centre, $7$ cm apart. Find the
radius of the circle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let the perpendicular distances from the centre be $d_1$ and $d_2$
($d_1 + d_2 = 7$). Use Pythagoras to relate each chord to the radius.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let radius be $r$. Then $r^2 = d_1^2 + 3^2 = d_1^2 + 9$ and
$r^2 = d_2^2 + 4^2 = d_2^2 + 16$. So $d_2^2 = d_1^2 - 7$. Together with
$d_1 + d_2 = 7$: substitute $d_2 = 7 - d_1$, get $(7 - d_1)^2 = d_1^2 -
7 \Rightarrow 49 - 14 d_1 = -7 \Rightarrow d_1 = 4$, so $d_2 = 3$.
$r = \sqrt{16 + 9} = 5$ cm.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The angle in a semicircle is:
(a) $45^\circ$ (b) $60^\circ$ (c) $90^\circ$ (d) $180^\circ$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(c) $90^\circ$** — Thales' theorem.
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** A chord of a circle of radius $10$ cm
subtends a right angle at the centre. Find the length of the chord.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The chord and two radii form a right triangle with the right angle at
the centre.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Length $= \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = 10\sqrt{2}$ cm.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** $ABCD$ is a cyclic quadrilateral with
$\angle A = 100^\circ$ and $\angle B = 70^\circ$. Find $\angle C$ and
$\angle D$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Opposite angles supplementary: $\angle C = 180 - 100 = 80^\circ$,
$\angle D = 180 - 70 = 110^\circ$. Check: $100 + 70 + 80 + 110 = 360$ ✓.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Prove that the perpendicular from the centre
of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Right triangles $\triangle OMA$ and $\triangle OMB$, with hypotenuse =
radius.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $OM \perp AB$, $M$ on $AB$. | Given. |
| 2. $OA = OB$. | Radii. |
| 3. $OM = OM$. | Common. |
| 4. $\angle OMA = \angle OMB = 90^\circ$. | Given. |
| 5. $\triangle OMA \cong \triangle OMB$. | RHS. |
| 6. $AM = MB$. | CPCT. So $M$ is the midpoint of $AB$. $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 9, Problem 9.3 (chord
distance, similar to W1). **Previous-year pointer:** CBSE Class 9 sample
paper "prove perpendicular from centre bisects chord" (similar to
5-mark).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"The angle subtended at the centre equals the angle subtended at
   the rim."** No — *centre angle is twice the rim angle* for the same
   arc.
2. **"Every quadrilateral inscribed in a circle has all four angles
   equal."** No — only opposite angles are *supplementary*. They need
   not be equal individually.
3. **"A chord is the same as a diameter."** A diameter is the longest
   chord; not every chord is a diameter.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **The perpendicular from the centre to a chord cuts it in half.**
   That single move converts almost every chord problem into a right
   triangle and Pythagoras.
2. **Centre angle is double the rim angle on the same arc.** Memorise
   this one sentence and most circle theorems fall out of it.
3. **A cyclic quadrilateral pairs its opposite angles to
   $180^\circ$.** That is why the rim of a circle keeps the four
   corners "balanced".

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you prove "perpendicular from centre bisects chord" without
   notes?
2. Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
3. Could you state the inscribed-angle theorem and its consequences
   (same segment, semicircle, cyclic quadrilateral) cold?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Heron's Formula**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* 2–5. The inscribed-angle theorem is
  the engine of this chapter.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.lines-and-angles`](../06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd) — angle
  facts, especially exterior-angle and triangle-sum.
- [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
  — radius, diameter, chord vocabulary.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.circles-tangents`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/11-circles-tangents/index.qmd)
  — tangent–radius, alternate-segment theorem.
- [`cbse10.areas-related-to-circles`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/12-areas-related-to-circles/index.qmd)
  — sector and segment areas.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles** (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus).
  Covers Sections 9.1–9.5: chord theorems, central vs inscribed angles,
  cyclic quadrilateral.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different angle):
  [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
  meets the circle as a shape; the *theorems* are this chapter's
  contribution.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Camera-frustum culling — deciding which objects
are visible — uses the angle subtended by an object at the camera. The
inscribed-angle theorem is the formal version of "things on the same
horizon look the same width regardless of where you are on the
horizon".
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Thales' theorem (Discover Problem 4) is the oldest
recorded named theorem, attributed to Thales of Miletus around 600 BCE.
It is also the key step in deriving the equation of a circle from
$x^2 + y^2 = r^2$ — the first place algebra and geometry truly fuse.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Kiselev (proof discipline), Russian
  problem-first (the inscribed-angle theorem is forced through Discover
  before *Name* states it), Better Explained (the "centre angle is
  double the rim" memorable phrasing).
:::
